subtitle.part1: UI/UX designer with 11 years of experience in IT:

axelnac.title: Redesign of the Live Logs section in AxelNAC
axelnac.description: Redesigned the interface for working with real-time logs based on user feedback — removed an unnecessary screen, simplified how information is presented, and added missing features.

logiq.title: Redesign of the main LogIQ section
logiq.description: Rethought and rebuilt the product's main section after the MVP with limited initial input. Received positive user feedback after release.

darts.title: Darts Tournament Management System
darts.description: Designed a complex system for running darts tournaments from scratch. Participated in the full development cycle from requirements gathering to design reviews of finished features.

adidas.title: Adidas — promo site / Pyrobattle 2023 contest
adidas.description: In 12 days I developed the concept and designed a landing page for a future Adidas promo site. The entry placed 49th out of 185.

experiences.tasks: Ideco NGFW — a multifunctional firewall with a web interface. I implement new scenarios and improve existing ones, maintain and enhance the design system, present my solutions internally, and conduct mockup reviews and design reviews of features after development.

LogIQ — a web app for monitoring and analysing system logs and handling security incidents. I implement new scenarios, improve existing ones, maintain and develop the design system, present my solutions within the team, and conduct design reviews after development.

items: Reworked the product's main section after the MVP: based on feedback the interface became simpler and more accessible to users; SUS increased by 18 points
Improved the design system: modernized key components and the token system, speeding up the team's work
Created UI guides and patterns, unified user flows and reduced time to create new features — UI bugs after design review decreased by 19%
Optimized the design review process: introduced JIRA for tracking issues and sped up reviews using templates
Built a Figma plugin for quickly finding common mistakes in mockups

experiences.tasks: Designed a tournament management system from scratch — a web application primarily used on desktop and partially on mobile. Conducted interviews and requirements gathering, designed user flows and the interface, and presented solutions to the client.

items: User flows became consistent and logical

items.title: Learning from Errors
items.text: About why form errors occur, why they frustrate users, how to reduce that frustration and help users avoid getting stuck in a flow.

items.title: How I Built a Figma Plugin Using ChatGPT
items.text: I share my experience creating a notes plugin with neural networks that feels native and supports category configuration.

axelnac.subtitle: Live Logs — AxelNAC section for monitoring real-time events. It is important that users can quickly and easily obtain and analyze information.

items.title: Improve the section's usability
items.text: The interface requires extra actions from the user: it's hard to find the necessary log files, filters are available only after data appears, and some settings have to be changed via terminal. This slows down analysis and complicates log work.

items.title: Improve information perception
items.text: The interface is overloaded with elements: panels and navigation occupy most of the screen and cannot be hidden, bright elements create visual noise. This makes it harder to focus on log content and find important events faster.

done.title: What I did
items: Conducted user interviews — found out what really annoys and what functions are missing
Conducted competitor analysis — selected several products for similar tasks, analyzed their pros and cons
Designed mockups and showed them to users — got feedback and made changes after discussion
Prepared mockups for handover to development

problems.title: Identified problems
items: On the first screen, you need to select log files for analysis — usually users are interested in logs of specific services and they don't know which files contain these logs. Since the journal file name does not match the service name, searching becomes difficult.
Navigation and options panel occupy most of the screen — no way to hide them. Especially sensitive for devices with small screens.
Filters are available only after matching lines appear in the logs — you have to see useless information until they appear to set the necessary filter.
Missing function to change logging level to debug from the interface — currently you have to switch to terminal to change, then return to the interface.
The interface is overloaded with bright elements that draw attention and interfere with focusing on important information.

before.title: Original scenario
before.1: The scenario consisted of two screens. On the first, the user selected files for analysis, filled in the search string for events, and started the session.
before.2: On the second screen, the user saw the logs window and the options panel. Options could be edited only after stopping the session.

after.title: Updated scenario
after.1: The section now consists of one page. All filters are available immediately — you can set what you need, start the monitoring session, and immediately start analysis, without being distracted by waiting and useless information. In all sections, the navigation panel is expanded by default, but when entering the Live Logs section, it automatically collapses, freeing up space for information.
after.2: After starting the session, parameters can be changed on the fly. The parameters block can be hidden to give more space to logs. Important information of error and warning levels is highlighted in color. A new function for highlighting individual words in logs has been added to see them faster. Bulky filter buttons have been replaced with more compact checkboxes — by default, the 5 most important or frequently used filters are shown, and the rest are available by clicking the 'Show all' button.
after.3: Control buttons are grouped by meaning (session management, content management, settings) and placed at the bottom of the screen, where users can easily reach them if necessary. Some buttons have been replaced with icons for clarity. Display settings are hidden in a separate window since they are used rarely. The 'Clear screen' button is intentionally placed aside to avoid accidentally erasing received data.

result.title: Result
result.1: The scenario became shorter and more logical, and the interface — simpler and cleaner. The task of enabling debug mode from the interface requires significant rework, so we added it to the backlog.

logiq.subtitle: LogIQ — a product for cybersecurity analysts who need speed and clear presentation of information for incident investigations

simplify.title: Simplify the growing interface
simplify.text: With new features the interface started accumulating elements chaotically — visual noise increased and information became harder to perceive.

reduce.title: Lower the product's entry barrier
reduce.text: Not all analysts know SQL and some are intimidated by the interface because it feels complex and 'heavy'.

increase.title: Increase the interface's informativeness
increase.text: The interface 'hides' information needed by analysts — they must remember or recall details, which distracts from their work.

items.0: Collected and analysed user feedback — what users dislike or find inconvenient
items.1: Conducted user interviews to learn how they use the product
items.2: Performed competitive analysis — selected systems for similar tasks and analysed their pros and cons
items.3: Designed the main section structure so it can be easily extended in the future
items.4: Redesigned the main section according to the new structure and improved the query parameter input flows

before.title: Before
before.main: Key information was hidden in the query string — there was no clarity about which events were currently shown on screen (expanding the query line didn't help those who don't know SQL). Buttons were placed without a clear system — it wasn't obvious what each did. Many colorful elements distracted attention.
before.constructor1: The query builder intimidated users with its size and the number of elements.
before.constructor2: To get results a user first had to click 'Apply' to set the query parameters, then 'Run' to get the results.

after.title: After

structure.title: New page structure
structure.text: In builder mode there is no SQL query line, yet all parameters are shown — now there's always clarity about which events are being analysed. Tags can be removed easily by clicking the cross. The interface is less colorful and airier, leaving emphasis only on critical controls — the focus shifted to the table data.

after.grouped: Information on the page is grouped and structured — buttons and other elements now have their 'place', so adding new ones won't be an issue.

modes.title: Two modes
modes.text1: Due to implementation specifics we decided to provide two separate modes for working with queries: 'Builder' and 'SQL line'.
modes.text2: The 'Builder' provides a visual interface for constructing queries, while the SQL line mode allows users to work directly with raw SQL text.

constructor.title: Builder mode
constructor.main1: The bulky builder was split into smaller parts — it is now presented as a toolbar since it's easier to solve one task at a time. Other builder blocks don't distract from the current task and no longer overwhelm users.
constructor.main2: All changes are applied immediately. To fetch new results just click 'Execute'.

fields.title: Field selection
fields.text: Field selection is now done in a compact dropdown where you can quickly find a field using search. Quick-select buttons for selecting all fields or default fields are also available. Selected fields appear at the top of the list for quick access. In the same window you can set a field alias or switch to an advanced mode where multiple aliases can be specified.

search.title: Entering search conditions
search.text: Entering a condition became a guided process where the system helps the user — knowing SQL is no longer required to use the product effectively. At any time the user can enter parameters with the mouse or switch to keyboard input.

sorting.title: Sorting
sorting.text: The sorting flow became shorter and more consistent — the dropdown now shows sortable fields immediately. For quick access, fields from the query are displayed at the top. When choosing a field the system suggests a commonly used sort direction by default. For example, users usually sort records by timestamp from newest to oldest.

result.title: Result
result.text: According to user feedback, the new interface became more intuitive and easier to use. Users noted that it's now easier to find features and work with analysis tools.
result.feedback1: From my side: clients during demos and pilots highly rate the functionality and maturity of the product, its UI, and its intuitiveness.
result.feedback2: Users really like the log management interface

darts.subtitle: The Chelyabinsk Darts Federation is a non-profit organization that promotes darts. Besides organising tournaments, they also provide training for children and adults. For tournaments the federation used a special system that lacked necessary tools. Additionally, the system owners required placing bets at tournaments, which is unacceptable for an NPO.

task.title: Task
task.text1: Develop a system to automate darts tournaments, minimize manual actions and reduce errors.
task.text2: ❤️ This work was completed as part of an assignment on procharity.ru.

items.0: Held a briefing with the client. Discussed how tournaments are generally run, organizer roles, common problems, shortcomings of the current system, and expectations for the new system.
items.1: Analysed the current system and studied the sport rules. Read user guides and went through the flow from creating a tournament to its completion. Documented current system features and identified key weaknesses: outdated design, hard to understand without documentation, and confusing flows.
items.2: Designed a system concept to meet user needs: defined main entities, their attributes, and user flows. Prepared mockups and presented them to the client.
items.3: Conducted onboarding for new members of the development team.
items.4: Performed design reviews of the completed system after development.

player.title: Creating a player
player.text1: A player is a core entity in the system. Creation starts from the players list. To avoid overwhelming the user, form fields are split into three meaningful tabs filled step-by-step. A stepper shows progress and required information.
player.text2: The form contains only the information necessary to participate in the tournament. Some fields are optional and do not block player creation, but missing information must be provided before the tournament starts.

tournament.title: Creating a tournament
tournament.text1: Tournament creation also starts from the list. A tournament has many parameters, so it contains more tabs. At the first stage the organizer has access to only the first two tabs — continuing requires knowing the main parameters and structure that affect match logic.
tournament.text2: The 'Structure' tab defaults to a mixed tournament system as the most commonly used option.
tournament.text3: The administrator can change the tournament before it actually starts. Once the tournament begins, fields on the 'Main' and 'Structure' tabs are locked to avoid breaking match formation logic.

scalability.title: Scalability
scalability.text: Pages use a universal template. As the system grows (for example, adding teams) it's easy to add buttons for new sections in the sidebar. Other elements like filters, search, tables, and tabs can be reused for new pages — this allows quickly assembling pages without breaking the familiar layout.

stages.title: Staged approach
stages.text: Complex and long flows and forms are broken into logical stages, which prevents overwhelming users and supports fast navigation in the system.

participants.title: Adding participants to a tournament
participants.text: The administrator can add players one by one or several at once. Players already added do not appear in the list.

documents.title: Checking documents before the tournament
documents.text: Before the tournament starts on site, the organizer checks players' documents. The registered participants table provides all necessary data for verification.

groups.title: Group formation
groups.text: The system randomly assigns players to groups. If needed, the organizer can swap players to balance group composition.

finish.title: Finishing the tournament
finish.text: When tallying results, the system ranks players according to points, but the administrator can still make adjustments. The top three places are usually individual; subsequent places may be grouped (for example, 4–8 without specifying exact positions).

informativeness.title: Informativeness
informativeness.text: At different stages the interface hints what is happening or what will happen next — lowering the entry barrier and reducing errors. Users also won't need to consult documentation to complete tasks.

familiarity.title: Familiar UX
familiarity.text: The match calculator is adapted for mobile devices used by judges to record results. Informational messages are shown depending on match status. The calculator interface resembles many familiar scorekeeping apps used by athletes.

result.title: Result
result.text1: The new system reduces manual work and the chance of errors as much as possible.

items.0: A player database was created that stores necessary data — no need to fill everything manually, and important information is highlighted.
items.1: The tournament creation and execution flow was redesigned to closely match how it works in reality.
items.2: The report generation stage was automated — preparing documents now requires minimal user involvement.

result.text2: Additionally, the system has other important characteristics that will make it easier to use and extend in the future.

adidas.subtitle: This project is a contest entry where I developed a concept and designed a landing page for an Adidas promo site in 12 days.

task.title: Task
task.text: Imagine all well-known IT entrepreneurs and their companies moved to Russia. Elon Musk, Zuckerberg and others launch businesses in regional cities. They repeat their success, but their products gain local flavour. In 12 days create a landing page or promo site design and submit it as a case on Dprofile.

concept.title: Idea and concept
concept.main1: If well-known companies relocate to Russia in the future, there will be opportunities to create high-tech products. Also consider that digitalisation is actively promoted in Russia.
concept.main2: I chose the major sports brand Adidas as a foreign brand that will produce clothing and footwear of the future. Besides its own production, Adidas will create unique items in collaboration with local companies.

ai.title: Use of neural networks
ai.text: With neural networks online stores will offer instant virtual try-ons in a couple of clicks and suggest outfits based on context.

ar.title: AR/VR adaptation
ar.text: AR/VR technologies will be widespread, changing patterns with new device types emerging.

landing.title: Landing
landing.text: On the main page the user selects gender 'men' or 'women'. You can also upload a photo to virtually 'try on' clothing on a specific person.

outfit.title: View full outfit and choose items
outfit.text: Like a game character, the user can customize their look by selecting different clothing elements and accessories. You can immediately see how selected items look on a model and the total cost of the full outfit.

specs.title: Selected item's specifications
specs.text: On a product page the user can see all characteristics of the selected item, including materials, sizes, and available colors.

animation.title: Interface animation
animation.text: The interface uses windows that appear to float in space, creating depth and multilayer effects. Sliding animations add dynamism and a futuristic feel.

feedback.title: Judges' comments
feedback.text1:
Hi, Anna! My name is Sergey, I'll be reviewing your work.
                        Very strong work. I liked how you implemented the clothing animation via Figma. It immediately reminded me of the Cyberpunk game at launch — I even preordered it on PS4 back then. Golden times. Overall I liked everything. I'd tweak the case presentation a bit: maybe rework and combine the first slides? But it's up to you. Great job, looking forward to requests.
feedback.text2: Cool idea, even if not entirely new. But clothes are rarely the main focus.
